screens.prompt: Your car's speedometer gives a single number for your speed *right now*. But speed is distance divided by time — and at a single instant, no time passes at all. So what is the speedometer actually measuring? Here's a toy version of the same puzzle: a toy car rolls down a ramp so that after $x$ seconds it has covered $y = x^2$ metres. Does the car's speed stay the same throughout the roll, the way a car cruising at a constant speed on a straight road would?
choices: Yes — the car's speed is the same at every instant, just like cruising at a constant speed
No — the car's speed changes from instant to instant
screens.reveal: Near $x = 0$ the car has barely started rolling and is almost stationary; further down the ramp it's moving fast. We define the car's **speed at an instant** as the gradient of the *tangent* to its distance-time graph — the straight line that just touches the curve at that exact moment. Unlike cruising at a constant speed, the car needs a different tangent (and so a different speed) at every instant. That's the resolution to the speedometer paradox: it's reading the gradient of your distance-time graph at this exact instant, not an average over any stretch of time.

screens.prompt: Drag the point along the car's distance-time graph until its speed at that instant reads **4 m/s**.
screens.successFeedback: That's it — the car is moving at exactly 4 m/s right around $x = 2$ seconds.
hints: Try moving the point further down the ramp, where the car has picked up more speed.
You're looking for the instant $x = 2$ seconds.
At $x = 2$ s, the car has rolled $y = x^2 = 4$ m — land the point there and read its speed.

screens.prompt: You just found the instant the car is moving at 4 m/s. What does that number actually tell you?

choices.text: The car's exact speed at that one instant
The car's average speed over the whole roll
choices.feedback: That's closer to what a chord between two far-apart points on the graph gives you — an average speed over a stretch of time, not the speed at one instant.

choices.text: How far the car has rolled by that instant
choices.feedback: That's just $y = x^2$ evaluated there — the distance travelled, not how fast that distance is changing.

screens.successFeedback: Exactly — the derivative gives the car's exact speed at one instant, never an average over a stretch of time. That's the speedometer paradox resolved: 'instantaneous speed' means the gradient of the distance-time graph at that exact point.

screens.prompt: To calculate that exact instantaneous speed (not just drag to it), take a second instant $Q$ on the graph, close to your instant $P$, and slide $Q$ toward $P$. As $Q$ gets closer and closer to $P$, what happens to the average speed between $P$ and $Q$ — the gradient of the chord (straight line) $PQ$?
choices: It gets closer and closer to the car's instantaneous speed at $P$
It stays the same, however close $Q$ gets
screens.reveal:
Take $P = (2, 4)$ on the car's graph $y = x^2$ — 2 seconds in, 4 metres rolled — and a nearby instant $Q = (2+h, (2+h)^2)$. The average speed between them is

$$
m_{PQ} = \frac{(2+h)^2 - 4}{(2+h)-2} = \frac{4h + h^2}{h} = 4 + h.
$$

As $h \to 0$, $4 + h \to 4$ — exactly the instantaneous speed you found by dragging. The same idea at $P = (1, 1)$ — 1 second in, 1 metre rolled — gives $m_{PQ} = 2 + h \to 2$. Both match a pattern: the car's speed at $x$ seconds is $2x$ m/s.

screens.prompt: Using that pattern — the car's speed at $x$ seconds is $2x$ m/s — how fast is it moving at $x = 3$ seconds?
screens.successFeedback: Right — $2 \times 3 = 6$ m/s. The next lesson makes this pattern exact and general, for any distance-time model, not just this one ramp.
hints: At $x = 2$ s the speed was $4 = 2 \times 2$ m/s. At $x = 1$ s it was $2 = 2 \times 1$ m/s.
Apply the same rule at $x = 3$: speed $= 2 \times 3$.
